鳥居・社殿と楠 |所在地 = [[佐賀県]][[神埼市]][[神埼町]]城原 |ISO = JP-41 |緯度度 = 33|緯度分 = 20|緯度秒 = 55.45 |経度度 =130|経度分 = 21|経度秒 = 54.07 |ラベル位置 = bottom |祭神 = [[日本武尊]]（1911年（明治44年）[[仁比山神社]]へ合祀） |神…」
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 白角折神社 |画像 = [[File:Oshitori Shrine torii, hall and trees in Kanzaki.jpg|200px|鳥居・社殿と楠]]
鳥居・社殿と楠 |所在地 = [[佐賀県]][[神埼市]][[神埼町]]城原 |ISO = JP-41 |緯度度 = 33|緯度分 = 20|緯度秒 = 55.45 |経度度 =130|経度分 = 21|経度秒 = 54.07 |ラベル位置 = bottom |祭神 = [[日本武尊]]（1911年（明治44年）[[仁比山神社]]へ合祀） |神体 = |社格 = [[国史見在社]] |創建 = 不明（[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]15年（873年）以前） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = （[[11月13日 (旧暦)|11月13日]]）{{R|市史1}} |神事 = 　 |地図 = Japan Saga }} [[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|枝分かれが盛んな「白角折神社のクス」全景]] '''白角折神社'''（おしとりじんじゃ）は、[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]][[神埼町]]城原字朝日にある[[神社]][[#市史1|『神埼市史』第1巻]]、532頁、544頁。境内の樹齢約1,000年の大[[楠]]は'''白角折神社のクス'''として佐賀県の[[天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|天然記念物]]に指定されている。 == 概要・歴史 == 創始は不明だが、『[[三代実録]]』の[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]15年（873年）[[9月16日 (旧暦)|9月16日]]の条に「授肥前国正六位上白角折神従五位下」とあって[[従五位下]]に叙せられている古社。東妙寺文書や櫛田神社文書に記載がみられるが、[[神埼荘]]の鎮守として[[櫛田宮]]および[[高志神社]]神埼市千代田町下板とともに三所大明神、三社宮と称された{{R|市史1}}[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「神埼郡」頁。（参考:[https://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7445055 JLogos]）[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「仁比山神社」頁。[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「櫛田宮」頁。{{Cite kotobank|word=白角折神社 |encyclopedia=平凡社「日本歴史地名大系」 |access-date=2024-02-20}}。3社は[[城原川]]流域に南北に並んでおり、白角折神社は山地から平地へ流れ出る場所にある{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1207 |title=白角折神社 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmFeatureDetail.aspx?feature_id=1 |title=肥前國風土記にみる神埼 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |date=2012-03-01 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 3社は[[免田]]を有しており、白角折神社については[[元徳]]元年（1329年）の文書に記載がある。こうした記録から、古くは白角折神社が3社の中心だった可能性もあるが、特に荘園とともに衰微していったと考えられる{{R|市史1|角川1|歴史地名}}[[#市史2|『神埼市史』第2巻]]、472-473頁。 [[明治]]4年（[[1871年]]）の神社調差出帳によれば[[明暦]]年間の火災で、他の伝承では[[慶応]]2年（[[1866年]]）6月の水害によって古い記録は失われたとされ、[[足利氏]]や[[少弐氏]]に関する文書があったという。1866年の水害では社殿も流失し、その後現在の拝殿と神殿が再建された{{R|市史1|市1}}。 [[1911年]]（明治44年）9月に日吉神社（同年10月に[[仁比山神社]]と改称）へ合祀され、神体も同社に移された。その一方で社殿・境内は残され、朝日地区の氏子らによって維持されるとともに、現在も[[夏祭り]]が催されている{{R|市史1|角川1}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmFeatureDetail.aspx?feature_id=2 |title=特集 白角折神社（おしとりじんじゃ） |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |date=2012-02-06 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 江戸時代までは[[神楽]]（能舞）が奉納されていた{{R|市1|市2}}。神殿には女神と男神の2体の[[神像]]を所蔵する。女神は[[貞和]]2年（1346年）の銘がある楠を用いた木像。男神は桐の一木造の像である{{R|市1}}。 参道には額束に「白角折宮」の銘を掲げる鳥居1基が建つ。柱は三本継で、[[元禄]]13年（[[1700年]]）建立。江戸時代には立っていたもう1基の鳥居は、片方の柱の根元部分のみが残存するが、その上面には[[盃状穴]]がみられる。このほか、石造物には[[鍋島綱茂]]の代の配分帳にて朝日村に知行地の記載がある鍋島庄兵衛の名を記したものもある{{R|市2}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1204 |title=白角折神社鳥居 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=2910 |title=白角折神社鳥居 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 「白角折」の名には、[[日本武尊]]が[[熊襲]]征伐のとき白角折神社の場所から的に向かって矢を射たという伝説に基づき、[[幣帛|幣]]作りの「オシトリ」部に由来するという説があり{{Refnest|古代の品部には織物を作った倭文部（しとりべ）がある{{Cite kotobank|word=倭文部 |encyclopedia=平凡社「世界大百科事典」 |access-date=2024-02-20}}。|group="注"}}、近隣の地名にある「的」も的作りの「イクハ」部に由来するとして両地名の関連が考えられている{{R|角川1|市1|市2}}。その他、西の国を制圧するときに鳥が白い角を持ってきて戦いが好転したことで「おしとり」に「白角折」の字を充てたという説もある{{R|市2}}。 境内は東西45メートル(m)・南北110 mで面積およそ5,000 平方メートル{{R|市史1}}。 == 白角折神社のクス == 古来から[[神木]]とされてきた推定樹齢1,000年の楠の古木。1965年（昭和40年）7月23日に佐賀県の天然記念物に指定されており、後に県の「さが名木100選」にも選定されている。樹高22 m、目通り幹回り10.5 m。絡み合い癒着した[[板根]]がみられ、盛り上がった根は高さ2mになり、根回りは29.3 mに及ぶ。小さな空洞はみられるものの樹勢は盛んで、東西に32 m・南北に26 mの枝張りがある。枝分かれが多く、根元からやや南に傾いた主幹は高さ8 m付近で四方に分岐する。高さ4 m付近から出る下枝が南方向へ大きく伸びており、特徴的である。[[マメヅタ]]、[[ノキシノブ]]、[[ネズミモチ]]などの[[着生]]もみられる{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031653/index.html#ki9 |title=県指定（天然記念物の部）01 |work=佐賀県文化財データベース |publisher=佐賀県文化課 |date=2020-06-25 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji00369053/index.html |title=佐賀県名木・古木 |work=佐賀県文化財データベース |publisher=佐賀県文化課 |date=2023-06-14 |accessdate=2023-08-10}}。 == 関連項目 == * [[仁比山神社]] * [[櫛田宮]] * [[佐賀県指定文化財一覧]] == 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1 |ref=角川 }} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=[[神埼市]] |year=2022 |month=3 |ncid=BC18582083 |ref=市史1 }} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=[[神埼市]] |year=2022 |month=3 |ncid=BC18582312 |ref=市史2 }} {{DEFAULTSORT:おしとりしんしや}} [[Category:佐賀県の神社]] [[Category:神埼市の歴史]] [[Category:神埼市の建築物]] [[Category:佐賀県指定天然記念物]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oshitori Shrine (白角折神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oshitori Shrine torii, hall and trees in Kanzaki.jpg|200px|Torii, shrine hall, and camphor tree]] | caption = Torii, shrine hall, and camphor tree | location = {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|33|20|55.45|N|130|21|54.07|E|type:landmark|display=inline}} | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}} (relocated to {{ill|Nihiyama Shrine|ja|仁比山神社}} in 1911) | founded = Unknown (prior to {{ill|Jōgan (Japan)|ja|貞観}} 15, 873 CE) | type = {{ill|Historical Shinto shrines listed in the Engishiki|ja|国史見在社}} | festivals = November 13 }}
[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]] '''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}, Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物}} of Saga Prefecture.
== Overview and History == The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘}}.
The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.
According to records from the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the {{ill|Meireki era|ja|明暦}} or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}} and {{ill|Shōni clan|ja|少弐氏}}. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.
In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}} performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to {{ill|Jōwa era|ja|貞和}} 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.
== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine == The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a {{ill|Sacred tree|ja|神木}}, is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Microsorum|ja|ノキシノブ}}, {{ill|Ligustrum|ja|ネズミモチ}}, and ferns grow on its surface.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Nihiyama Shrine|ja|仁比山神社}} * {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮}} * {{ill|List of cultural properties of Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県指定文化財一覧}}
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}}
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=Kadowaka Japan Place name Dictionary Compilation Committee |title={{ill|Kadowaka Japan Place name Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} 41: Saga Prefecture |publisher=Kadowaka Shoten |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1 }} * {{Cite book|editor=Kanzaki City History Compilation Committee |title=Kanzaki City History, Volume 1 (Nature, Folklore, Stone Monuments) |publisher=Kanzaki City |year=2022 |month=March }} * {{Cite book|editor=Kanzaki City History Compilation Committee |title=Kanzaki City History, Volume 2 (Ancient, Medieval, and Early Modern Periods) |publisher=Kanzaki City |year=2022 |month=March }}

Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Microsorum|ja|ノキシノブ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ligustrum|ja|ネズミモチ|12=draft}}, and ferns grow on its surface.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Nihiyama Shrine|ja|仁比山神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of cultural properties of Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県指定文化財一覧|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oshitori Shrine (白角折神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oshitori Shrine torii, hall and trees in Kanzaki.jpg|200px|Torii, shrine hall, and camphor tree]] | caption = Torii, shrine hall, and camphor tree | location = {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|33|20|55.45|N|130|21|54.07|E|type:landmark|display=inline}} | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}} (relocated to {{ill|Nihiyama Shrine|ja|仁比山神社|12=draft}} in 1911) | founded = Unknown (prior to {{ill|Jōgan (Japan)|ja|貞観|12=draft}} 15, 873 CE) | type = {{ill|Historical Shinto shrines listed in the Engishiki|ja|国史見在社|12=draft}} | festivals = November 13 }}
[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]] '''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft}}, Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=draft}} of Saga Prefecture.
== Overview and History == The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=draft}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=draft}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=draft}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=draft}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=draft}}.
The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=draft}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=draft}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.
According to records from the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the {{ill|Meireki era|ja|明暦|12=draft}} or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shōni clan|ja|少弐氏|12=draft}}. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.
In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft}} performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to {{ill|Jōwa era|ja|貞和|12=draft}} 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.
== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine == The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a {{ill|Sacred tree|ja|神木|12=draft}}, is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Microsorum|ja|ノキシノブ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ligustrum|ja|ネズミモチ|12=draft}}, and ferns grow on its surface.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Nihiyama Shrine|ja|仁比山神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of cultural properties of Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県指定文化財一覧|12=draft}}
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}}
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=Kadowaka Japan Place name Dictionary Compilation Committee |title={{ill|Kadowaka Japan Place name Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典|12=draft}} 41: Saga Prefecture |publisher=Kadowaka Shoten |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1 }} * {{Cite book|editor=Kanzaki City History Compilation Committee |title=Kanzaki City History, Volume 1 (Nature, Folklore, Stone Monuments) |publisher=Kanzaki City |year=2022 |month=March }} * {{Cite book|editor=Kanzaki City History Compilation Committee |title=Kanzaki City History, Volume 2 (Ancient, Medieval, and Early Modern Periods) |publisher=Kanzaki City |year=2022 |month=March }}

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 白角折神社 |画像 = [[File:Oshitori Shrine torii, hall and trees in Kanzaki.jpg|200px|鳥居・社殿と楠]]
鳥居・社殿と楠 |所在地 = [[佐賀県]][[神埼市]][[神埼町]]城原 |ISO = JP-41 |緯度度 = 33|緯度分 = 20|緯度秒 = 55.45 |経度度 =130|経度分 = 21|経度秒 = 54.07 |ラベル位置 = bottom |祭神 = [[日本武尊]]（1911年（明治44年）[[仁比山神社]]へ合祀） |神体 = |社格 = [[国史見在社]] |創建 = 不明（[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]15年（873年）以前） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = （[[11月13日 (旧暦)|11月13日]]）{{R|市史1}} |神事 = 　 |地図 = Japan Saga }} [[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|枝分かれが盛んな「白角折神社のクス」全景]] '''白角折神社'''（おしとりじんじゃ）は、[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]][[神埼町]]城原字朝日にある[[神社]][[#市史1|『神埼市史』第1巻]]、532頁、544頁。境内の樹齢約1,000年の大[[楠]]は'''白角折神社のクス'''として佐賀県の[[天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|天然記念物]]に指定されている。 == 概要・歴史 == 創始は不明だが、『[[三代実録]]』の[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]15年（[[873年]]）[[9月16日 (旧暦)|9月16日]]の条に「授肥前国正六位上白角折神従五位下」とあって[[従五位下]]に叙せられている古社。東妙寺文書や櫛田神社文書に記載がみられるが、[[神埼荘]]の鎮守として[[櫛田宮]]および[[高志神社]]神埼市千代田町下板とともに三所大明神、三社宮と称された{{R|市史1}}[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「神埼郡」頁。（参考:[https://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7445055 JLogos]）[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「仁比山神社」頁。[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「櫛田宮」頁。{{Cite kotobank|word=白角折神社 |encyclopedia=平凡社「日本歴史地名大系」 |access-date=2024-02-20}}。3社は[[城原川]]流域に南北に並んでおり、白角折神社は山地から平地へ流れ出る場所にある{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1207 |title=白角折神社 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmFeatureDetail.aspx?feature_id=1 |title=肥前國風土記にみる神埼 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |date=2012-03-01 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 3社は[[免田]]を有しており、白角折神社については[[元徳]]元年（[[1329年]]）の文書に記載がある。こうした記録から、古くは白角折神社が3社の中心だった可能性もあるが、特に荘園とともに衰微していったと考えられる{{R|市史1|角川1|歴史地名}}[[#市史2|『神埼市史』第2巻]]、472-473頁。 [[明治]]4年（[[1871年]]）の神社調差出帳によれば[[明暦]]年間の火災で、他の伝承では[[慶応]]2年（[[1866年]]）6月の水害によって古い記録は失われたとされ、[[足利氏]]や[[少弐氏]]に関する文書があったという。1866年の水害では社殿も流失し、その後現在の拝殿と神殿が再建された{{R|市史1|市1}}。 [[1911年]]（明治44年）9月に日吉神社（同年10月に[[仁比山神社]]と改称）へ合祀され、神体も同社に移された。その一方で社殿・境内は残され、朝日地区の氏子らによって維持されるとともに、現在も[[夏祭り]]が催されている{{R|市史1|角川1}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmFeatureDetail.aspx?feature_id=2 |title=特集 白角折神社（おしとりじんじゃ） |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |date=2012-02-06 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 江戸時代までは[[神楽]]（能舞）が奉納されていた{{R|市1|市2}}。神殿には女神と男神の2体の[[神像]]を所蔵する。女神は[[貞和]]2年（[[1346年]]）の銘がある楠を用いた木像。男神は桐の一木造の像である{{R|市1}}。 参道には額束に「白角折宮」の銘を掲げる鳥居1基が建つ。柱は三本継で、[[元禄]]13年（[[1700年]]）建立。江戸時代には立っていたもう1基の鳥居は、片方の柱の根元部分のみが残存するが、その上面には[[盃状穴]]がみられる。このほか、石造物には[[鍋島綱茂]]の代の配分帳にて朝日村に知行地の記載がある鍋島庄兵衛の名を記したものもある{{R|市2}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1204 |title=白角折神社鳥居 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=2910 |title=白角折神社鳥居 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 「白角折」の名には、[[日本武尊]]が[[熊襲]]征伐のとき白角折神社の場所から的に向かって矢を射たという伝説に基づき、[[幣帛|幣]]作りの「オシトリ」部に由来するという説があり{{Refnest|古代の品部には織物を作った倭文部（しとりべ）がある{{Cite kotobank|word=倭文部 |encyclopedia=平凡社「世界大百科事典」 |access-date=2024-02-20}}。|group="注"}}、近隣の地名にある「的」も的作りの「イクハ」部に由来するとして両地名の関連が考えられている{{R|角川1|市1|市2}}。その他、西の国を制圧するときに鳥が白い角を持ってきて戦いが好転したことで「おしとり」に「白角折」の字を充てたという説もある{{R|市2}}。 境内は東西45メートル(m)・南北110 mで面積およそ5,000 平方メートル{{R|市史1}}。 == 白角折神社のクス == 古来から[[神木]]とされてきた推定樹齢1,000年の楠の古木。1965年（昭和40年）7月23日に佐賀県の天然記念物に指定されており、後に県の「さが名木100選」にも選定されている。樹高22 m、目通り幹回り10.5 m。絡み合い癒着した[[板根]]がみられ、盛り上がった根は高さ2mになり、根回りは29.3 mに及ぶ。小さな空洞はみられるものの樹勢は盛んで、東西に32 m・南北に26 mの枝張りがある。枝分かれが多く、根元からやや南に傾いた主幹は高さ8 m付近で四方に分岐する。高さ4 m付近から出る下枝が南方向へ大きく伸びており、特徴的である。[[マメヅタ]]、[[ノキシノブ]]、[[ネズミモチ]]などの[[着生]]もみられる{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031653/index.html#ki9 |title=県指定（天然記念物の部）01 |work=佐賀県文化財データベース |publisher=佐賀県文化課 |date=2020-06-25 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji00369053/index.html |title=佐賀県名木・古木 |work=佐賀県文化財データベース |publisher=佐賀県文化課 |date=2023-06-14 |accessdate=2023-08-10}}。 == 関連項目 == * [[仁比山神社]] * [[櫛田宮]] * [[佐賀県指定文化財一覧]] == 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1 |ref=角川 }} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=[[神埼市]] |year=2022 |month=3 |ncid=BC18582083 |ref=市史1 }} * {{Cite revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[白角折神社]] to [[Oshitori Shrine]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 白角折神社 |画像 = [[File:Oshitori Shrine torii, hall and trees in Kanzaki.jpg|200px|鳥居・社殿と楠]]
鳥居・社殿と楠 |所在地 = [[佐賀県]][[神埼市]][[神埼町]]城原 |ISO = JP-41 |緯度度 = 33|緯度分 = 20|緯度秒 = 55.45 |経度度 =130|経度分 = 21|経度秒 = 54.07 |ラベル位置 = bottom |祭神 = [[日本武尊]]（1911年（明治44年）[[仁比山神社]]へ合祀） |神体 = |社格 = [[国史見在社]] |創建 = 不明（[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]15年（873年）以前） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = （[[11月13日 (旧暦)|11月13日]]）{{R|市史1}} |神事 = 　 |地図 = Japan Saga }} [[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|枝分かれが盛んな「白角折神社のクス」全景]] '''白角折神社'''（おしとりじんじゃ）は、[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]][[神埼町]]城原字朝日にある[[神社]][[#市史1|『神埼市史』第1巻]]、532頁、544頁。境内の樹齢約1,000年の大[[楠]]は'''白角折神社のクス'''として佐賀県の[[天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|天然記念物]]に指定されている。 == 概要・歴史 == 創始は不明だが、『[[三代実録]]』の[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]15年（[[873年]]）[[9月16日 (旧暦)|9月16日]]の条に「授肥前国正六位上白角折神従五位下」とあって[[従五位下]]に叙せられている古社。東妙寺文書や櫛田神社文書に記載がみられるが、[[神埼荘]]の鎮守として[[櫛田宮]]および[[高志神社]]神埼市千代田町下板とともに三所大明神、三社宮と称された{{R|市史1}}[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「神埼郡」頁。（参考:[https://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7445055 JLogos]）[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「仁比山神社」頁。[[#角川|『角川日本地名大辞典』]]、「櫛田宮」頁。{{Cite kotobank|word=白角折神社 |encyclopedia=平凡社「日本歴史地名大系」 |access-date=2024-02-20}}。3社は[[城原川]]流域に南北に並んでおり、白角折神社は山地から平地へ流れ出る場所にある{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1207 |title=白角折神社 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmFeatureDetail.aspx?feature_id=1 |title=肥前國風土記にみる神埼 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |date=2012-03-01 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 3社は[[免田]]を有しており、白角折神社については[[元徳]]元年（[[1329年]]）の文書に記載がある。こうした記録から、古くは白角折神社が3社の中心だった可能性もあるが、特に荘園とともに衰微していったと考えられる{{R|市史1|角川1|歴史地名}}[[#市史2|『神埼市史』第2巻]]、472-473頁。 [[明治]]4年（[[1871年]]）の神社調差出帳によれば[[明暦]]年間の火災で、他の伝承では[[慶応]]2年（[[1866年]]）6月の水害によって古い記録は失われたとされ、[[足利氏]]や[[少弐氏]]に関する文書があったという。1866年の水害では社殿も流失し、その後現在の拝殿と神殿が再建された{{R|市史1|市1}}。 [[1911年]]（明治44年）9月に日吉神社（同年10月に[[仁比山神社]]と改称）へ合祀され、神体も同社に移された。その一方で社殿・境内は残され、朝日地区の氏子らによって維持されるとともに、現在も[[夏祭り]]が催されている{{R|市史1|角川1}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmFeatureDetail.aspx?feature_id=2 |title=特集 白角折神社（おしとりじんじゃ） |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |date=2012-02-06 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 江戸時代までは[[神楽]]（能舞）が奉納されていた{{R|市1|市2}}。神殿には女神と男神の2体の[[神像]]を所蔵する。女神は[[貞和]]2年（[[1346年]]）の銘がある楠を用いた木像。男神は桐の一木造の像である{{R|市1}}。 参道には額束に「白角折宮」の銘を掲げる鳥居1基が建つ。柱は三本継で、[[元禄]]13年（[[1700年]]）建立。江戸時代には立っていたもう1基の鳥居は、片方の柱の根元部分のみが残存するが、その上面には[[盃状穴]]がみられる。このほか、石造物には[[鍋島綱茂]]の代の配分帳にて朝日村に知行地の記載がある鍋島庄兵衛の名を記したものもある{{R|市2}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1204 |title=白角折神社鳥居 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=2910 |title=白角折神社鳥居 |website=神埼デジタルミュージアム「かんざき@NAVI」 |publisher=神埼市 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}。 「白角折」の名には、[[日本武尊]]が[[熊襲]]征伐のとき白角折神社の場所から的に向かって矢を射たという伝説に基づき、[[幣帛|幣]]作りの「オシトリ」部に由来するという説があり{{Refnest|古代の品部には織物を作った倭文部（しとりべ）がある{{Cite kotobank|word=倭文部 |encyclopedia=平凡社「世界大百科事典」 |access-date=2024-02-20}}。|group="注"}}、近隣の地名にある「的」も的作りの「イクハ」部に由来するとして両地名の関連が考えられている{{R|角川1|市1|市2}}。その他、西の国を制圧するときに鳥が白い角を持ってきて戦いが好転したことで「おしとり」に「白角折」の字を充てたという説もある{{R|市2}}。 境内は東西45メートル(m)・南北110 mで面積およそ5,000 平方メートル{{R|市史1}}。 == 白角折神社のクス == 古来から[[神木]]とされてきた推定樹齢1,000年の楠の古木。1965年（昭和40年）7月23日に佐賀県の天然記念物に指定されており、後に県の「さが名木100選」にも選定されている。樹高22 m、目通り幹回り10.5 m。絡み合い癒着した[[板根]]がみられ、盛り上がった根は高さ2mになり、根回りは29.3 mに及ぶ。小さな空洞はみられるものの樹勢は盛んで、東西に32 m・南北に26 mの枝張りがある。枝分かれが多く、根元からやや南に傾いた主幹は高さ8 m付近で四方に分岐する。高さ4 m付近から出る下枝が南方向へ大きく伸びており、特徴的である。[[マメヅタ]]、[[ノキシノブ]]、[[ネズミモチ]]などの[[着生]]もみられる{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031653/index.html#ki9 |title=県指定（天然記念物の部）01 |work=佐賀県文化財データベース |publisher=佐賀県文化課 |date=2020-06-25 |accessdate=2024-02-20}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji00369053/index.html |title=佐賀県名木・古木 |work=佐賀県文化財データベース |publisher=佐賀県文化課 |date=2023-06-14 |accessdate=2023-08-10}}。 == 関連項目 == * [[仁比山神社]] * [[櫛田宮]] * [[佐賀県指定文化財一覧]] == 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1 |ref=角川 }} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=[[神埼市]] |year=2022 |month=3 |ncid=BC18582083 |ref=市史1 }} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=[[神埼市]] |year=2022 |month=3 |ncid=BC18582312 |ref=市史2 }} {{DEFAULTSORT:おしとりしんしや}} [[Category:佐賀県の神社]] [[Category:神埼市の歴史]] [[Category:神埼市の建築物]] [[Category:佐賀県指定天然記念物]]

| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
|image           = File:Oshitori Shrine torii, hall and trees in Kanzaki.jpg

| type            = {{ill|Historical Shinto shrines listed in the Engishiki|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical Shinto shrines listed in the Engishiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}
| festivals       = November 13
| prefecture= {{ill|Kanzaki|qid=Q990392}}
}}

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]], [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]], Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|qq=|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

== Related Sites ==
* {{ill|Niiyama Shrine|lt=Niiyama Shrine|lt=Nihiyama Shrine|ja|仁比山神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihiyama Shrine|qq=}}
* {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}}
* {{ill|List of cultural properties of Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of cultural properties of Saga Prefecture|qq=|1=List of cultural properties of Saga Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11384711}}

== References ==
=== Notes ===
{{Reflist|group="注"}}

=== Sources ===
{{Reflist}}

== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|editor=Kadowaka Japan Place name Dictionary Compilation Committee |title={{ill|Kadowaka Japan Place name Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadowaka Japan Place name Dictionary|qq=|1=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4093011}} 41: Saga Prefecture |publisher=Kadowaka Shoten |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1 }}
* {{Cite book|editor=Kanzaki City History Compilation Committee |title=Kanzaki City History, Volume 1 (Nature, Folklore, Stone Monuments) |publisher=Kanzaki City |year=2022 |month=March }}
* {{Cite book|editor=Kanzaki City History Compilation Committee |title=Kanzaki City History, Volume 2 (Ancient, Medieval, and Early Modern Periods) |publisher=Kanzaki City |year=2022 |month=March }}

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]], [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]], Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|qq=|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]], [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]], Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]], [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]], Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

== Related Sites ==

* {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}}

== Bibliography ==

* {{Cite book|editor=Kanzaki City History Compilation Committee |title=Kanzaki City History, Volume 2 (Ancient, Medieval, and Early Modern Periods) |publisher=Kanzaki City |year=2022 |month=March }}

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]], [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]], Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]], [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]], Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]], [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]], Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the [[:en:Meireki|Meireki era]] or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] and [[:en:Shōni clan|Shōni clan]]. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa era]] 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a [[:en:Shinboku|Sacred tree]], is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Ligustrum]], and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.
