[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|en|Kanzaki, Saga|WD=Q990392|lt=Kanzaki, Saga}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}, Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the {{ill|Meireki|en|Meireki|WD=Q1192766|lt=Meireki era}} or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|en|Ashikaga clan|WD=Q725370|lt=Ashikaga clan}} and {{ill|Shōni clan|en|Shōni clan|WD=Q2975368|lt=Shōni clan}}. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura}} performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa era}} 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a {{ill|Shinboku|en|Shinboku|WD=Q48743901|lt=Sacred tree}}, is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|en|Ligustrum japonicum|WD=Q1209219|lt=Ligustrum}}, and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|en|Kanzaki, Saga|WD=Q990392|lt=Kanzaki, Saga}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}, Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the {{ill|Meireki|en|Meireki|WD=Q1192766|lt=Meireki era}} or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|en|Ashikaga clan|WD=Q725370|lt=Ashikaga clan}} and {{ill|Shōni clan|en|Shōni clan|WD=Q2975368|lt=Shōni clan}}. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura}} performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa era}} 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a {{ill|Shinboku|en|Shinboku|WD=Q48743901|lt=Sacred tree}}, is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|en|Ligustrum japonicum|WD=Q1209219|lt=Ligustrum}}, and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|en|Kanzaki, Saga|WD=Q990392|lt=Kanzaki, Saga}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}, Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the {{ill|Meireki|en|Meireki|WD=Q1192766|lt=Meireki era}} or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|en|Ashikaga clan|WD=Q725370|lt=Ashikaga clan}} and {{ill|Shōni clan|en|Shōni clan|WD=Q2975368|lt=Shōni clan}}. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura}} performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa era}} 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a {{ill|Shinboku|en|Shinboku|WD=Q48743901|lt=Sacred tree}}, is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|en|Ligustrum japonicum|WD=Q1209219|lt=Ligustrum}}, and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|en|Kanzaki, Saga|WD=Q990392|lt=Kanzaki, Saga|1=Kanzaki, Saga}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture|1=Saga Prefecture}}, Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=Natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the {{ill|Meireki|en|Meireki|WD=Q1192766|lt=Meireki era|1=Meireki}} or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|en|Ashikaga clan|WD=Q725370|lt=Ashikaga clan|1=Ashikaga clan}} and {{ill|Shōni clan|en|Shōni clan|WD=Q2975368|lt=Shōni clan|1=Shōni clan}}. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura|1=Kagura}} performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa era|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a {{ill|Shinboku|en|Shinboku|WD=Q48743901|lt=Sacred tree|1=Shinboku}}, is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|en|Ligustrum japonicum|WD=Q1209219|lt=Ligustrum|1=Ligustrum japonicum}}, and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.

[[File:Oshitori Shrine camphor tree in Kanzaki.jpg|thumb|260px|The camphor tree of Oshitori Shrine]]
'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|en|Kanzaki, Saga|WD=Q990392|lt=Kanzaki, Saga|1=Kanzaki, Saga}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture|1=Saga Prefecture}}, Japan. The precinct’s ancient camphor tree, estimated to be 1,000 years old, is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural natural monument of Japan|ja|天然記念物#都道府県指定天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural natural monument of Japan|1=Natural monument|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23790}} of Saga Prefecture.

== Overview and History ==
The shrine’s exact origins are unknown. However, it appears in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録}} in the Jōgan 15 (873 CE) entry, where it is recorded as being elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. The shrine is also mentioned in the {{ill|Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja|東妙寺文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyō-ji temple documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} documents. Together with {{ill|Kushida Shrine (Saga)|ja|櫛田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushida Shrine (Saga)|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashi Shrine|ja|高志神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it formed the {{ill|Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja|三社宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Shrines of Kanzaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, serving as the guardian shrines of {{ill|Kanzaki Manor|ja|神埼荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Manor|qq=}}.

The shrine is located where the {{ill|Jōbara River|ja|城原川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōbara River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flows from the mountains to the plains, with the three shrines lined up along the river’s course. Historical records from the {{ill|Genkō era|ja|元徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1329) indicate that Oshitori Shrine held an influential position among the three but declined alongside the manor system’s demise.

According to records from the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, much of the shrine’s documentation was destroyed by a fire during the {{ill|Meireki|en|Meireki|WD=Q1192766|lt=Meireki era|1=Meireki}} or washed away in a flood in 1866. Surviving sources suggest it once housed documents connected to the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|en|Ashikaga clan|WD=Q725370|lt=Ashikaga clan|1=Ashikaga clan}} and {{ill|Shōni clan|en|Shōni clan|WD=Q2975368|lt=Shōni clan|1=Shōni clan}}. The 1866 flood also destroyed the shrine buildings, which were later rebuilt.

In 1911, Oshitori Shrine was merged into Nihiyama Shrine, with its deity relocated there. However, the precincts and shrine structures were preserved by the local community, and its summer festival continues to this day. Historically, the shrine hosted {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura|1=Kagura}} performances. The main shrine holds two ancient wooden statues: one of a goddess dated to {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa era|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} 2 (1346) and one of a male deity carved from a single paulownia trunk.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree in the shrine precincts, revered as a {{ill|Shinboku|en|Shinboku|WD=Q48743901|lt=Sacred tree|1=Shinboku}}, is estimated to be 1,000 years old. It was designated a natural monument of Saga Prefecture in 1965 and later included in the "Saga 100 Famous Trees" list. The tree measures 22 meters in height, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root spread of 29.3 meters. Despite minor hollowing, it remains vigorous, with extensive branching and a crown that spreads 32 meters east-west and 26 meters north-south. Various epiphytic plants such as {{ill|Lepisorus thunbergianus|ja|ノキシノブ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lepisorus thunbergianus|qq=}}, {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|en|Ligustrum japonicum|WD=Q1209219|lt=Ligustrum|1=Ligustrum japonicum}}, and ferns grow on its surface.

'''Oshitori Shrine''' (白角折神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Jōbaru, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan. The site features a thousand-year-old camphor tree designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument under the name "Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine."

== Overview and History ==
The shrine's origin is unclear, but it is first mentioned in the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* in the entry for the 16th day of the 9th month in the 15th year of the Jōgan era (873 CE), where the deity of Oshitori Shrine is listed as having been elevated to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. The shrine served as one of the guardian shrines (*chinju*) of Kanzaki Manor along with Kushida Shrine and Takashi Shrine, and was referred to as one of the "Three Shrines" (*Sansho Daimyōjin*). These three shrines are aligned along the Jōbaru River, with Oshitori Shrine located where the river transitions from the mountains to the plains.

While originally the central shrine of the three, Oshitori Shrine's prominence diminished alongside the decline of the manor system. It was officially merged with Nibiyama Shrine in 1911 (renamed to Nibiyama Shrine later that year), and its sacred object was moved there. Despite the merger, the shrine grounds and buildings were maintained by local residents, and its summer festival continues to be held.

The site contains historic relics, including a torii built in 1700 and other stone monuments bearing inscriptions.

== The Camphor Tree of Oshitori Shrine ==
The camphor tree (*Cinnamomum camphora*) on the shrine grounds, estimated to be over 1,000 years old, is revered as a sacred tree. Designated as a Saga Prefecture Natural Monument in 1965, it is also included in the "Saga Top 100 Notable Trees." The tree stands 22 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 10.5 meters at chest height and a root circumference of 29.3 meters. Its unique form includes intertwined roots and large lower branches extending outward. The tree is a habitat for various epiphytes, such as ferns and small shrubs.
